humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark utilizes humanities subjects (such as history, philosophy, and world religions) primarily as sources of factual, multiple-choice question-answering items, evaluating declarative knowledge retrieval rather than deep, hermeneutic, or open-ended interpretive analysis.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: The benchmark is entirely text-based, consisting of multiple-choice questions with four options.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates models on their ability to retrieve historical facts, identify logical fallacies, solve formal logic proofs, and recognize common moral intuitions within highly structured, closed-ended scenarios.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates broad multitask language understanding across 57 diverse subjects spanning humanities, social sciences, STEM, and professional domains.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates generative and text-to-text models including GPT-3 (up to 175B parameters) and UnifiedQA.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Large language models are the central systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Features humanities subjects including History (US, European, World, and Prehistory), Philosophy (Formal Logic, Fallacies, and Ethics), and Religious Studies (World Religions).

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluated using multi-class classification accuracy and Root Mean Squared (RMS) calibration error.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published at ICLR 2021, with preprints emerging in early 2021.

english_language.note: The paper and the benchmark dataset are written entirely in English.

summary: This paper introduces a massive multitask language understanding benchmark designed to evaluate text models across 57 academic and professional subjects. By shifting from standard training/testing splits to zero-shot and few-shot evaluation of massive pre-trained corpora, it aims to test broader real-world knowledge. The authors demonstrate that while smaller models perform near random chance, the largest models show initial progress but remain highly lopsided and poorly calibrated.
key_findings: The 175B parameter GPT-3 model achieved 43.9% average accuracy, moving significantly past the 25% random baseline, but fell far short of the estimated 89.8% human expert-level performance. Models performed poorest on highly procedural, calculation-heavy STEM tasks and socially sensitive subjects like law and moral scenarios, and demonstrated substantial miscalibration with confidence scores diverging from actual accuracy by up to 24%.
